overallInterpretation: The demo presents a comprehensive GPU compute architecture document for an ECS engine, covering data flow (CPU-to-GPU upload, GPU dispatch, GPU-to-CPU readback), a detailed component authority model (physics authority with intent components, authority guards, authority categories), and numerous design decisions (lazy buffer creation, grid-based vs sort-based broadphase, pipeline phase integration, WGSL codegen strategy). Two supporting artifacts demonstrate working GpuContext lifecycle (buffer pool, dirty tracking, authority guards) and WGSL code generation for three kernels (movement, particle, physics). Together these artifacts constitute an architecture document walkthrough with data flow, authority model, and design decisions.

artifacts.interpretation: A full architecture document covering: (1) data flow between CPU SoA TypedArrays and GPU storage buffers via upload/dispatch/readback, (2) pipeline phase integration showing where GPU systems slot into preUpdate/update/postUpdate, (3) GpuContext interface and lifecycle with lazy buffer creation, (4) GpuKernelDef DSL for defining compute kernels, (5) WGSL code generation from kernel definitions, (6) component authority model detailing physics authority with intent components (GpuForce, GpuImpulse, GpuTeleport), authority categories by entity type, and dev-mode authority guards, (7) multi-pass dispatch for physics, (8) edge cases (no WebGPU, buffer resize, entity destruction, f64 downcast), and (9) file structure. Design decisions are documented with rationale and rejected alternatives citing reference implementations (TypeGPU, bevy_hanabi, bevy_gpu_compute, GPU Gems, Unity GPU Physics, Godot, Bevy+Rapier).

Terminal output demonstrating GpuContext functionality: creating a context with a mocked WebGPU device, registering 3 components producing 8 buffers in the pool, buffer growth when adding 1000 entities, dirty tracking (cpuDirty/gpuDirty sets), GPU-authoritative component configuration, write claim registration with authority guard enforcement (correctly throwing on violation, allowing non-guarded writes), and clean context destruction.

Terminal output showing WGSL code generation for three kernels: (1) a movement kernel with 8 bindings reading Velocity and writing Transform, (2) a particle integration kernel with 14 bindings including age/color fade, and (3) a physics integration kernel with 20 bindings handling teleport, force, impulse, and position integration. All three kernels generated successfully, demonstrating the codegen pipeline produces valid WGSL with correct binding layouts, storage access modes, and dispatch guards.
